titleStmt.title: The Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive

respStmt.orgName: University of South Carolina Provost Humanities Grants
University of South Carolina Department of English
Center for Digital Humanities at the University of South Carolina
Magellan Scholar Program at the University of South Carolina
National Society of the Daughters of the American Revolution
Carnegie-Whitney Grant from the American Library Association

availability.p: Periodicals and books published before 1 January 1923 are in the public domain. If re-publishing, quoting from, or reproducing images from this digital archive, we request that you cite it as follows: Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive, Center for Digital Humanities, University of South Carolina.

Periodicals and books published on or after 1 January 1923 may be protected by U.S. Copyright Law. All attempts to secure permission for republishing post-1923 material in this archive have been made. The names, logos, and identifying images, as well as the content of the articles, remain the property of the periodicals and/or of the authors and artists themselves. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder. Archival material published in this archive is used with permission of the institution or library that holds it. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder.
Responsibility for any use of the contents of this digital archive rests exclusively with the user.

bibl.title: A Terrible Country. Covered with Skeletons

div.p: On the lonely wind-swept Island of San Nicholas, 80 miles off the coast of Ventura County, where as far back as the memory of man runs, hundreds of skeletons have dotted the valleys and hillsides, local archæologists are now gathering the relics of a strange extinct race. The island is ten miles long and oval in shape, being four miles across [at] the widest part. Strange utensils of serpentine, sandstone and steatite are found there among the human bones, and the island and its erstwhile inhabitants have a history so curious that it is difficult to comprehend. In 1835 the Padres of Santa Barbara, learning that there were but 16 of the strange Indian race then living there, determined to rescue them from the island. They went over in a sloop and succeeded, as they thought, in getting all on board. At the last moment an Indian woman returned for her child, and one of the frequent storms of the channel islands springing up, the sloop was driven away without her. The sloop went on the rocks of Point Conception and all on board were lost. Sixteen years later Captain George Nideyer and two men went from the coast on a sloop to hunt otter off San Nicholas. On landing, they were, like Crusoe, astonished to discover human footprints in the sand. They saw no one, however, and a storm compelled them to put to sea. It was two years thereafter, that the adventurous captain revolving in his mind the sight of the footprints in the uncanny island, determined to go and discover and bring over the lonely woman of whom he had vaguely heard. Men accompanied him, and at length they found on the surf-beaten shore a woman with long tawny hair, dressed in a queer garb of colored birdskins, and scraping, with a bone knife, the blubber of a seal. They surrounded and approached her stealthily, and although suddenly confronted, she did not appear in the least afraid, but smiled, and then suddenly falling on her knees, prayed to the sun. The wild woman offered no objection when by signs she was made to understand that she was to go with them in the boat. They reached Santa Barbara across the rough sea, and the first thing the Indian woman saw was a man riding a horse. She had never seen [n]or heard of any object like it, and thought the man and horse were one, and she knelt on the shore and offered her devotions to it. Two weeks after the last inhabitant of rock-ribbed tempest-tossed San Nicholas died from eating food to which she was unaccustomed, furnished by her rescuers, and the curtain fell on her strange race for ever. Wild dogs had eaten her child.
